ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fruit tea beverage class; this record represents a Singaporean/Taiwanese-style sweetened fruit tea with brewed tea base and mixed fruit pieces. 'closest_parent' match type used as it is a specific variant of the general class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKM covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific code for sweetened fruit tea with mixed fruit pieces. 'closest_parent' match type used at category level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for sweetened fruit tea beverage. SNOMED CT coverage for prepared beverages of this type is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of fruit tea is approximately 500 ml (500 g), equivalent to a large cup as typically sold at Singaporean bubble tea and fruit tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beverages, tea, ready-to-drink, sweetened, with fruit flavour (SR Legacy); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for fruit juice components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Singaporean/Taiwanese-style sweetened fruit tea with mixed fruit pieces exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for sweetened ready-to-drink fruit-flavoured tea beverages (closest available approved-source analogue). Energy 40 kcal/100g, carbohydrate 10g/100g, sugar 9g/100g are consistent with a lightly sweetened fruit tea at standard dilution. Protein 0.2g/100g and fat 0.1g/100g are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for similar beverages. Vitamin C 6mg/100g is plausible given fresh fruit content and consistent with USDA data for fruit-containing beverages. Input values from the GrabFood listing (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained where consistent with the USDA adapted reference. Singapore HPB Foodo was consulted but does not contain a specific entry for this beverage type; USDA FoodData Central is used as the primary approved source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate: sweetened fruit juice and ready-to-drink sweetened tea beverages GI range 50–65; University of Sydney GI Database entries for fruit juice and sweetened tea beverages.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean/Taiwanese-style sweetened fruit tea. GI estimated at 55 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened fruit juice and ready-to-drink sweetened tea beverages, which cluster in the 50–65 range. A GI of 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low GI category (<=55) per the University of Sydney classification (low: <=55, medium: 56–69, high: >=70). Sugar content of 9g/100g with minimal fibre (0.3g/100g) is consistent with this estimate. Glycemic load per 500g serving = (55 × 50g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 27.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9g/100g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage; 45g sugar per standard 500ml serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this beverage due to its high sugar content (9g/100g, 45g per serving), as sugar-sweetened beverages independently raise blood pressure risk through insulin-driven sodium retention and weight gain; request an unsweetened or 0%-sugar version as a safer alternative (AHA 2018).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9g sugar/100g and negligible dietary fibre (0.3g/100g), causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form, as 9g sugar/100g with only 0.3g fibre/100g constitutes a sugar-sweetened beverage that causes rapid glucose spikes; a 500ml serving delivers approximately 45g of rapidly absorbed sugar with no fibre buffering, and an unsweetened version (0% sugar) should be requested if consumption is desired.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar load (45g per 500ml serving) contributing to hypertriglyceridaemia and excess caloric intake; low saturated fat and sodium are positive factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this beverage due to its high added sugar content (9g/100g, 45g per 500ml serving), which is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk; the low sodium (2mg/100g) and negligible saturated fat (0.02g/100g) are favourable, but the sugar load outweighs these benefits in a standard serving, and an unsweetened version is strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; fructose-based sweeteners associated with elevated uric acid
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the sweetened version of this beverage as the high sugar content (9g/100g) promotes inflammatory mediators and fructose-based sweeteners are associated with elevated uric acid; an unsweetened version would allow the tea polyphenols and fruit-derived vitamin C (6mg/100g) to provide genuine anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fruit Tea (水果茶, Shuǐguǒ chá) is a popular Singaporean and Taiwanese-style cold beverage consisting of a brewed tea base — typically green or black tea — mixed with a variety of fresh or preserved fruit pieces and fruit juices, and sweetened with sugar syrup or fructose. The drink is served chilled or over ice and is widely available at bubble tea shops, fruit tea stalls, and food delivery platforms across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The flavour profile is refreshing, lightly tangy, and sweet, with the tea providing a subtle astringent backdrop to the fruity sweetness. Common fruit additions include strawberry, kiwi, passion fruit, lemon, orange, and grape. Per 100g, this beverage provides approximately 40 kcal, 10g carbohydrate (of which 9g is sugar), 0.2g protein, 0.1g fat, 0.3g dietary fibre, 2mg sodium, and 6mg vitamin C. A standard 500ml serving delivers approximately 200 kcal and 45g of sugar, making it a high-sugar beverage. Clinically, the high sugar content (9g/100g) with negligible fibre makes this a sugar-sweetened beverage that is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard sweetened form. An unsweetened or reduced-sugar version significantly improves its health profile. The tea base contributes polyphenols and the fruit pieces contribute vitamin C and minor antioxidants. This beverage is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fruit tea?
Is fruit tea suitable for hypertension?
How much sugar is in fruit tea?
What is the GI of fruit tea?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Singaporean/Taiwanese-style sweetened fruit tea exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for sweetened ready-to-drink fruit-flavoured tea beverages and are consistent with the input GrabFood listing (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). The values are plausible and internally consistent but cannot be verified against an exact approved-source entry for this specific product. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for sweetened fruit juice and tea beverages.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this sweetened fruit tea beverage is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific beverage type is not reported in the adapted USDA SR Legacy source entries consulted. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available in the adapted USDA SR Legacy source for sweetened fruit tea beverages. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no trans fats are expected in a brewed tea and fresh fruit beverage; consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for similar beverages. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero. 6. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but the record provenance is ai_generated from GrabFood; that URL is not an approved nutrient source and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the primary approved source. Singapore HPB Foodo was consulted but does not contain a specific entry for this beverage type. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for sweetened fruit juice and ready-to-drink sweetened tea beverages; direct GI measurement for this specific product is not available. GI 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low GI category per University of Sydney classification (low <=55); gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. 8. The name '宝岛经典水果茶' (Bǎodǎo jīngdiǎn shuǐguǒ chá) translates as 'Treasure Island Classic Fruit Tea', referencing a Taiwanese-origin brand/style popular in Singapore. 9. Aliases self-check: all values in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — confirmed consistent.
